entries.ref: Torah – Noah and the three colours of humanity
entries.text: In this Masowe Torah teaching, the three sons of Noah are presented as the roots of three lines of humanity. Shem is linked to the brown peoples of the middle lands, Japheth to the lighter peoples of the north, and Ham to the dark-skinned peoples of Africa. The dark skin of Ham’s line is described as a permanent sign that cannot be washed away, not something a person can change by their own effort.

entries.ref: Torah – Ham and the work of uncleanness
entries.text: The Masowe reading of the flood story says that Ham came to be tied to the work of uncleanness in the ark – dealing with the waste and the lowest tasks – and that this work marked his line. Some teachers in this tradition connect this to the later history of black people being forced into servitude, saying that the world pushed the sons of Ham into the same low work again and again.

entries.ref: Torah – ‘Can the Ethiopian change his skin?’
entries.text: The verse where God asks if the Ethiopian can change his skin is read here as a witness that blackness is a fixed sign, not an insult. The teaching says that just as the leopard cannot remove its spots, the sons of Africa cannot wash away the colour that God Himself wrote into their bodies. It becomes a reminder that vanhu vatema are a chosen people with a visible covenant on their skin.

entries.ref: Torah – The land of Cush and the anointing of the Messiah
entries.text: Using the writings of Enoch and other old witnesses, this Masowe Torah places special weight on the land of Cush, Ethiopia and the Nile region. It teaches that from this belt of Africa God prepared a dry and chosen land, and that from there the anointing of the Messiah would rise. The rivers, animals and landforms of Africa are treated as living proof that God walked there when He set His plan for redemption.

entries.ref: Torah – Vanhu vatema as keepers of the Torah
entries.text: The book teaches that the Torah was never only for one small Middle-Eastern nation, but that black people are at the heart of its story. Vanhu vatema are described as the hidden keepers of God’s Law, scattered and oppressed but still carrying the memory of the commandments. The scrolls say that while others changed the writings to fit their empires, God kept the true covenant alive among the poor and the dark-skinned.

entries.ref: Torah – The long slavery and the new earth
entries.text: Masowe Torah links the long slavery of black people to the ancient warnings of the Torah, where a nation that forgets the covenant would be scattered, carried away and used without mercy. It then speaks of a time when God will remember them, break the yoke and open a nyika itsva – a new earth – where former slaves stand as a people with their own land, law and dignity restored.

entries.ref: Torah – Baba Johane and the return of Torah to Africa
entries.text: Within this tradition, Baba Johane of Masowe is presented as a prophet raised from among vanhu vatema to return the Torah to its rightful owners. He calls people to leave the empty religion of colonisers and to hear God in the open places, under the sun and on the bare ground. His message is that the God of the Torah is the God of Africa, and that the black child is not cursed but called.

entries.ref: Torah – Africa as a centre of God’s map
entries.text: The Masowe Torah teaching draws a map where the rivers, deserts and mountains of Africa match the paths of the early scriptures. Cush, Ethiopia and the Nile are treated not as footnotes but as central places in God’s plan. The book argues that the true Holy Land must be understood with Africa at the centre, because that is where God wrote both the land and the people into His story.

entries.ref: Torah – Judgment is by covenant, not by colour
entries.text: Even while it honours vanhu vatema as a chosen people, the Masowe Torah warns that judgment does not fall on skin but on covenant. A black person who rejects truth can fall under the same darkness as any other, and a person of another nation who bows to God’s law can be received. The measure of a person in this teaching is not race but whether they walk with the God of the Torah in humility.
